paper_title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
citation_sentences: The EBSD results depicted in Fig. 3 demonstrate that a single α (Body-centered cubic, BCC) phase formed in the solution-treated Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels. To clarify the microstructural origin of the rapid aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels, EBSD characterization was performed on these alloys subjected to different aging treatments. Fig. 4 presents the EBSD results of the 15Al alloy after different aging treatments. After aging at 525 ◦C for 3 h, it is evident that a significant amount of β-Mn had precipitated (85.8 %), with only a small amount of α matrix remaining untransformed. The formation of β-Mn led to the increase in hardness from 253.4 HV at the solid-solution state to 872.0 HV, indicating the strong hardening effect of the β-Mn phase. After aging at $5 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 6 h, the α matrix had almost entirely transformed into β-Mn. Furthermore, a new fine BCC phase was present within the β-Mn. Following aging at 650 ◦C for 3 h, the untransformed α regions were no longer observed, and a new FCC phase was distributed in β-Mn.

citation_sentences: For the 17Al and 19Al alloys, the precipitation rate of β-Mn was noticeably slowed down when compared to the 15Al alloy. Fig. 5 shows that after aging the 17Al alloy at $5 2 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 10 h, only a small amount of β-Mn (20.5 %) formed along the α grain boundaries and within the α grains. In addition, some small α-Mn particles presented in the α matrix, conistent with our previous study [15]. In samples aged at $5 7 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 6 h and at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 10 h, the α matrix had almost completely transformed into β-Mn. Besides, a small amount of new precipitates with BCC (Red highlighted) and FCC (Pink highlighted) crystalline structures were present within the β-Mn. Fig. 6 indicates that after aging the 19Al alloy at $5 2 5 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 10 h, only a small amount of β-Mn precipitated (12.1 %), resulting in a minimal increase in hardness. It is noteworthy that in the sample aged at a high temperature of 650 ◦C for 6 h, the α matrix had almost completely transformed into β-Mn, and a great amount of new BCC phase was observed within the β-Mn.

citation_sentences: Based on the EBSD results, it can be inferred that the obtained ultrahigh hardness originates from the extensive formation of β-Mn. Fig. 7 presents the SE images of the 15Al alloy subjected to different aging treatments. Based on the previous study [15], the SE images can be used to identify the precipitation of secondary phases such as α-Mn and β-Mn phases in the aged samples. The α-Mn precipitates were dispersed in the α matrix as fine particles. The short rod-shaped β-Mn phase precipitated along α grain boundaries and the interior of α grains, and gradually grew

The combined microstructure observations and nanoindentation results indicated that the key to achieving ultra-high hardness in Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels through aging lies in the complete phase transformation from the α matrix to β-Mn. In this study, we investigated the effects of Al contents on the aging hardening responses of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels. The results showed that the increase in Al contents decreased the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn. When the Al content was 15.0 at.%, β-Mn precipitated rapidly during the aging processes at temperatures of $5 2 5 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and above. For example, within 3 h of aging at 525 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the α matrix had been almost completely transformed into β-Mn (Fig. 7). However, for the 17Al and 19Al alloys, this transformation was only completed after 6 h and 10 h when aging at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ respectively (Figs. 8 and 9). Both the precipitation and growth of β-Mn involve the process of atomic diffusion. The decrease in the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn with increasing Al contents is explained as follows: 1) The β-Mn is a Mn-rich phase, and its nucleation and growth must exclude large amounts of other atoms to the surroundings. As shown in Fig. 15, the β-Mn is rich in Mn while poor in Al and Cu. In this case, the increase in Al

citation_sentences: Figs. 8 and 9 show the SE images of the 17Al and 19Al alloys after different aging treatments, respectively. It is evident that the increase in Al content slowed down the precipitation kinetics of β-Mn during the low-temperature aging processes. When 17Al and 19Al alloys were aged at $5 2 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the precipitation of β-Mn was very slow as compared to the 15Al alloy, and only a small amount of β-Mn was observed even after 10 h of aging. The β-Mn primarily precipitated along α grain boundaries, while a small amount of short rod-shaped β-Mn was also observed within the α grains. A ${ \bf 5 5 0 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the α matrix had nearly fully transformed into β-Mn in the 17Al alloy after 6 h of aging. As for the 19Al alloy, it required 10 h. After 1 h of aging at ${ 5 7 5 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ a small amount of β-Mn precipitated along α grain boundaries and within α grains in the 17Al alloy, while β-Mn in the 19Al alloy could only be observed along α grain boundaries. Even extending the aging time to 3 h, the untransformed α matrix was still observed in the 19Al alloy. However, at temperatures of 600 ◦C and above, the α had almost completely transformed into β-Mn in both alloys after just 1 h of aging. Numerous fine precipitates distributed within the β-Mn were also observed in the 17Al and 19Al alloys after the α matrix had fully transformed into β-Mn. Based on the SE results, the 15Al alloy aged at $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, 17Al alloy aged at $5 7 5 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 1 h, and 19Al alloy aged at 550 ◦C for 6 h were selected for XRD tests, and the results are shown in Fig. 10. Both α-Mn and β-Mn diffraction peaks were found in these XRD patterns. This indicates that α-Mn and β-Mn were coprecipitated during aging before the α matrix fully transformed to β-Mn.

paper_title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
citation_sentences: to further elucidate the microstructures of these alloys with ultra-high hardness. As shown in Fig. 11 (a, b), the α matrix completely disappeared and abundant near equiaxed β-Mn formed in the 15Al alloy after aging at $6 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 6 h. Besides, some fine particles are evenly distributed at the interfaces between the β-Mn. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns shown in Fig. 11 (b) indicate that the fine particles exhibited an FCC crystalline structure, with FCC twins frequently observed within the particles. This is consistent with the EBSD result shown in Figs. 4, 5 and 6. Fig. 11 (c) shows that the FCC phase was rich in Cu, depleted in Fe and Mn, and slightly poor in Al. Therefore, these precipitates were referred to as the FCC Cu-rich phase hereafter. In addition, the β-Mn was poor in Cu according to Fig. 11 (c).

paper_title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
citation_sentences: when the indenter was pressed into the FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases, indicating that the intrinsic hardness of these precipitates were all lower than that of the β-Mn. The error in micro-hardness increased with increasing Al content, which laterally indicates that the content of these FCC and BCC precipitates also increased with the increase in Al content. We selected the areas where the nanoindentation indents did not contact the FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases to calculate the intrinsic hardness of the β-Mn. Accordingly, the β-Mn in hardest samples of 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys showed the average intrinsic hardnesses of 12.1 ± 0.3 GPa, 12.2 ± 0.1 GPa, and 12.5 ± 0.4 GPa, respectively. Because of the high intrinsic hardness of β-Mn, the alloys exhibited rapid hardening response during the aging treatments and achieved ultra-high hardness. Additionally, the chemical compositions of β-Mn in the three samples were measured by SME-EDS, and the results are shown in Fig. 14. In the hardest samples of 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys, the Al contents in the β-Mn were 14.3 at.%, 16.3 at.%, and 17.5 at.%, respectively. However, the contents of other elements in the three alloys were very close to each other. These results indicate that the increase in Al content slightly enhanced the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn in 15Al, 17Al, and 19Al alloys through solid-solution strengthening. However, as can be seen in Fig. 13 (b), the FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases between the β-Mn interfaces gradually increased with increasing Al content. Since the intrinsic hardness of these precipitates was lower than the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn, in this case, it resulted in the overall hardness showing a pattern of decreasing with increasing Al content.

of BCC stabilizing element Al around the interfaces of β-Mn [25]. The Cu-rich phase was formed due to the limited solubility of Cu in Fe-based alloys and the expelling of Cu from the β-Mn during its precipitation and growth [15,34]. The microstructure evolutions during aging treatments of the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels followed this sequence: ${ \mathfrak { x } } \to { \mathfrak { x } } +$ FCC Cu-rich phase + α-Mn + β-Mn + BCC phase→ β-Mn + BCC phase + FCC Cu-rich phase. The precipitates between β-Mn interfaces in the ultra-hard alloys gradually increase with the increase in Al content (Fig. 13). Furthermore, the EBSD results (Figs. 4, 5 and 6) show that the content of the FCC Cu-rich phase between β-Mn interfaces decreased gradually with the increase of the Al content.

According to the nanoindentation results, the increase in Al content would lead to the decrease in the micro-hardness of ultra-hard Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels (Fig. 13). Although the addition of Al enhanced the intrinsic hardness of the β-Mn through solid-solution strengthening, this enhancement was minimal as reflected by a marginal increase in the intrinsic hardness of the β-Mn [29]. Besides, the increase in Al content led to the increase of the content of FCC Cu-rich phase and BCC phase between the β-Mn interfaces. It is evident that both the intrinsic hardnesses of FCC Cu-rich and BCC phase were all significantly lower than that of β-Mn. The ultra-hard Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels were composed of β-Mn, as well as the FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases distributed between their interfaces. As a result, the hardness depended not only on the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn, but also on the absolute volume fraction of β-Mn. Elevated Al content led to the increase of FCC Cu-rich phase and BCC phase between the β-Mn interfaces, resulting in the decrease in the absolute volume fraction of β-Mn. Taking the EBSD results of the three alloys aged at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ as an example, the volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC phases of the 15Al alloy after aging at 650 ◦C for 3 h was 3.9 %, and the Vickers hardness of this sample was 852.3 HV. The volume fraction of these precipitates of the 17Al alloy after aging at 650 ◦C for 10 h was $4 . 7 ~ \% ,$ and the average Vickers hardness of the alloy was 833.2 HV. However, after the 19Al alloy aged at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 6 h, the volume fraction of FCC Cu-rich and BCC precipitates reached 6.5 %, and its Vickers hardness was only 810.9 HV. Ultimately, this led to the decrease in the overall micro-hardness as well as the highest Vickers hardness value of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with increasing Al content. In addition, the $K _ { \mathrm { I C } }$ of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu lowdensity steels decreased with increasing Al content. Firstly, the intrinsic hardness of β-Mn gradually increased with increasing Al content, and the $K _ { \mathrm { I C } }$ should be deteriorating accordingly. Moreover, the FCC phase exhibited relatively low intrinsic hardness but is more ductile, while the BCC phase is usually harder than the FCC phase [35]. Therefore, the decrease in ductile FCC Cu-rich phase due to increasing Al content led to the decrease in fracture toughness of ultra-hard alloys. The above two reasons together contributed to the decrease in the $K _ { \mathrm { I C } }$ of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with increasing Al content.

caption: Fig. 16. Specific hardness (hardness/density, HV/ρ) versus density among our Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels and various low-density steels, other steels (e.g., stainless steels, high-speed steels, ferritic/martensitic steels, carbon steels, etc.), pure iron, and medium/high entropy alloys.

paper_title: Effects of Al contents on aging hardening behaviors of Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high specific hardness
citation_sentences: In this study, we achieved exceptionally wide adjustable hardness values, ranging from 253.4 HV to 965.6 HV in the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu lowdensity steels through simple aging treatments. Notably, different hardness levels were obtained in these alloys by controlling aging parameters, thereby meeting various practical application requirements. Fig. 16 compares the specific hardness (hardness/density, HV/ρ) of our Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with various low-density steels, other types of steels, pure iron, and medium/high entropy alloys (M/HEAs) [17,18,35–73]. The Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels not only exhibit ultra-high hardness across a wide range of flexibility but also boast the advantage of low density. The hardness of existing steels and M/HEAs generally does not exceed 800 HV and coupled with the higher densities. This results in their specific hardness usually below $1 2 0 \ { \mathrm { H V } } { \cdot } { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 } / { \mathrm { g } } .$ In contrast, our Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels achieve a maximum specific hardness exceeding 140 $\scriptstyle { \mathrm { H V } } \cdot { \mathrm { c m } } ^ { 3 } / { \mathrm { g } }$ while maintaining low density. Furthermore, the Fe-Mn-Al-Cu low-density steels with ultra-high hardness also demonstrate acceptable fracture toughness. For instance, the 15Al alloy maintains a fracture toughness of 8.6 MPa⋅m1/2 even at a high hardness level of 965.5 HV. This represents a significant breakthrough in the research on high-performance low-density steels.
